title: The Cadillac (1)

jokes.text: Old people in Florida, they drive slow... and they sit low. Right? That is their motto.
jokes.setup: Jerry's opening stand-up about Florida driving habits
jokes.punchline: The rhythm and specificity of 'drive slow and sit low' as their actual motto
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational comedy with strong rhythm and specificity. The 'motto' framing elevates it beyond basic stereotype.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: In Florida, they have a motto for driving: 'Drive slow and sit low.' That's it. That's the whole thing. 'Drive slow and sit low.' Like they're in a low-rider in Miami Beach. They're doing 25 miles an hour in a Buick, sitting so low you can't even see them over the steering wheel. Just two hands and a head floating above the dashboard.

jokes.text: The state flag of Florida should be like... a steering wheel with a hat and two knuckles on it.
jokes.setup: Continuation of Florida elderly driver observation
jokes.punchline: Vivid visual image of the stereotypical elderly driver reduced to just essential elements
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual comedy that works on multiple levels. The specificity of 'two knuckles' is perfect detail work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what an elderly driver looks like? It's just two hands on a steering wheel. That's it. Just these two gnarled hands, gripping at nine and three, knuckles white, veins popping out like a road map of New Jersey.

jokes.text: And that left-turn signal on... from when they left the house that morning. Right? That's a legal turn in Florida. It's known as an eventual left.
jokes.setup: The perpetually-on turn signal observation
jokes.punchline: 'Eventual left' as an official term
jokes.explanation: 'Eventual left' is a brilliant neologism that perfectly captures the absurdity. Highly quotable Seinfeld coinage.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice people who leave their turn signal on? They're just driving along... (makes gesture) ...and they never turn. So I'm like, okay, you're gonna turn eventually. Maybe not today, maybe not tomorrow, but eventually you're gonna turn. I call it 'eventual left.' It's an official term now.

jokes.text: They just feel like; 'Well, I'm old, and I'm coming back. I survived. Let's see if you can.'
jokes.setup: Old people backing out without looking
jokes.punchline: The darkly competitive mindset behind reckless elderly driving
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that rings true. The survival-of-the-fittest mentality is a clever psychological insight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice old people backing out of parking spots? They don't look. They just put it in reverse and go. It's like a game to them. 'I'm gonna back out, and if there's a car there, that's YOUR problem for being behind me.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They've got this competitive thing going. Like, 'I've been driving for sixty years. I know what's behind me. I can feel it.' No you can't! You can't feel a Honda Civic!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And they're MAD if you honk. Like you've insulted them personally. 'How DARE you honk at me! I'm old! I've EARNED the right to destroy property!'

jokes.text: This is unbelievable... My God, you're rich.
jokes.setup: Kramer seeing Jerry's comedy check
jokes.punchline: Kramer's over-the-top amazement at the amount
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction humor. Sets up the money subplot but isn't particularly inventive on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Holy cow! Jerry, this is a lot of money!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not that much.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Not that much?! I've never seen a check with this many zeros!

jokes.text: I just don't think I can talk to you anymore. I feel inferior.
jokes.setup: Kramer learning about Jerry's wealth
jokes.punchline: Immediate declaration that their friendship is over due to money
jokes.explanation: Good character-based humor. Kramer's extreme reaction to social dynamics is true to form.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. You're rich? You're rich and you never told me?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not rich.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You have a cleaning lady!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, but that doesn't make me—

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: We're done. Our friendship is over.

jokes.text: I was thinking of donating a large portion of it to charity. - Really? - No.
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting charitable giving
jokes.punchline: Immediate honest 'No'
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with economy. Jerry's honesty about his selfishness is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You should give to charity.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Who was Pippi Longstocking? - Pippi Longstocking? I don't know. Did she have anything to do with Hitler? - Hitler? Maybe.
jokes.setup: Random trivia question about children's character
jokes.punchline: George's bizarre leap to Hitler connection
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd non-sequitur. The randomness feels earned because it's so specifically George-like in its paranoid logic.

jokes.text: You're married to the eye guy. - Also ear, nose and throat. - Nose. What's the worst that could happen to a nose? What does it get, stuffed?
jokes.setup: Discussion of ENT doctor specialties
jokes.punchline: George's dismissive view of nose problems as trivial
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's tendency to minimize others' expertise and problems.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, an ear, nose, and throat doctor... what's the big deal? It's not like they're doing brain surgery. They're just looking up your nose.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: George, some people have serious nasal problems.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Serious nasal problems? It's a nose! You breathe through it. If you can't breathe through it, you're sick. You get over it. Nobody needs a specialist for a nose.

jokes.text: You'd be perfect for her. She loves quirky, funny guys. - Bald? - Loves bald.
jokes.setup: Katy describing George's appeal to her friend
jokes.punchline: George immediately focusing on his baldness insecurity
jokes.explanation: Classic George insecurity moment. His immediate leap to his biggest fear is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Katy
dialogue.line: He's just so confident and self-assured.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Really? You think so? Because I've been thinking about getting a hair transplant. I mean, look at this. It's just not what it used to be.

jokes.text: Who is she? - Marisa Tomei. - The actress? - Yeah.
jokes.setup: Buildup to revealing the mystery woman
jokes.punchline: It's a famous actress
jokes.explanation: Good reveal moment that sets up the episode's main George subplot. The celebrity element raises the stakes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So who is this mystery woman you've been seeing?

dialogue.speaker: Katy
dialogue.line: I can't tell you, it's a surprise.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Come on, just give me a hint. Is she someone I know?

dialogue.speaker: Katy
dialogue.line: It's a famous actress.

jokes.text: Marisa Tomei's sitting home, Elaine. Why didn't you tell me Katy was friends with Marisa?
jokes.setup: George learning about the Marisa Tomei connection
jokes.punchline: His immediate assumption that this information should have been shared
jokes.explanation: George's self-centered worldview on display - why wouldn't Elaine organize her friendships around his romantic prospects?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, Marisa Tomei? You know Marisa Tomei?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How is this the first I'm hearing about this? This is information I needed to know!

jokes.text: I'm a very good boy.
jokes.setup: Jerry talking about buying his father a car
jokes.punchline: Adult man calling himself a 'very good boy'
jokes.explanation: Jerry's infantile self-description is amusingly incongruous with his age and success.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm buying my father a car. He's very excited about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I said, 'Dad, you have to be a very good boy.' And he's like, 'I will be a very good boy.' An adult man! Calling himself a very good boy!

jokes.text: Seems like a reasonable question. That's all I'm saying. I would have asked her.
jokes.setup: Jerry fixating on how Katy knows Marisa Tomei
jokes.punchline: His stubborn insistence that this was the obvious question to ask
jokes.explanation: Jerry's obsessive need to understand social connections is perfectly in character and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. How do you know Marisa Tomei?

dialogue.speaker: Katy
dialogue.line: I met her at a party.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A party? That's it? That's the whole story? No, no, no. I need more. This is the first thing you should have told me. This is the OBVIOUS question to ask someone. 'How do you know Marisa Tomei?' It's right up there with 'What's your name?' and 'Where are you from?'

jokes.text: Do you know the odds of me being anyone's type? I have never been anyone's type.
jokes.setup: George processing that he might be someone's type
jokes.punchline: His statistical analysis of his romantic appeal
jokes.explanation: Excellent George moment. The mathematical framing of his romantic failures is both pathetic and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm someone's type? I'm a type? What type am I? I'm not a type. Types are good-looking guys, athletes, guys with money. I'm a bald guy with a tire store.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. Maybe I'm the 'short, stocky, bald guy' type. There's gotta be women out there who are into that. Like, maybe one in a thousand.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: One in a thousand. That means there's thousands of them! There's thousands of women out there who want me!

jokes.text: She loves short, stocky, balding funnymen. - I notice you threw 'stocky' in. - Yeah, what the hell.
jokes.setup: George describing his type according to Marisa
jokes.punchline: Jerry catching George's self-flattering addition and George's casual admission
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing George's self-deception and Jerry's observational skills. 'What the hell' is perfect George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She said I was her type.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your type? What are you, a font?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, I added that.

jokes.text: If 50 years ago someone had fixed me up with Katharine Hepburn. Same thing. - Now there's a match, you and Katharine Hepburn.
jokes.setup: George comparing Marisa Tomei to classic Hollywood royalty
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic assessment of that hypothetical pairing
jokes.explanation: Good reality check from Jerry. The visual of George with Katharine Hepburn is absurdly funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She's like a young Audrey Hepburn. She's got that grace, that elegance...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, and if she married a guy who looked like a young Marlon Brando, their kids would be beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Of course, if she married you, they'd look like a young Danny DeVito.

jokes.text: Something you can really put the lipstick on. - Too bad you're engaged. - Yeah, too bad, too bad.
jokes.setup: George getting carried away describing Marisa's lips
jokes.punchline: Jerry's reality check followed by George's conflicted response
jokes.explanation: The awkwardness of George's lip fixation plus his obvious internal conflict about being engaged creates good cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Her lips... they're like two little sausages.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you're losing it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but I can't help myself!

jokes.text: This is no good.
jokes.setup: George alone with his thoughts after Jerry leaves
jokes.punchline: His understated realization that he's in trouble
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective moment showing George's dawning awareness of his moral predicament.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh no.

jokes.text: Well, I guess the cable man doesn't like to be kept waiting. - Do you remember what they did to me 10 years ago?
jokes.setup: Elaine noting Jerry's casual attitude toward the waiting cable guy
jokes.punchline: Jerry's revelation that this is revenge
jokes.explanation: Great setup for Jerry's revenge subplot. The 10-year grudge is perfectly petty and in character.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're not even worried about the cable guy coming?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, why should I be?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He could be here any time in the next four hours!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So you have to sit around and wait for him!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't mind.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You don't mind? Most people would go crazy!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not most people. Besides, this is revenge.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Revenge? For what?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: For all those times cable guys made me wait for them. Now it's their turn to wait for me.

jokes.text: Looks like the shoe is on the other foot, doesn't it? - I've never seen you like this. - You don't wanna get on my bad side.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining his power over the cable company
jokes.punchline: His menacing warning to Elaine
jokes.explanation: Jerry's rare moment of petty vindictiveness is funny because it's so out of character yet completely justified.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I have power over the cable company. They know I can leave anytime.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's great, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, you don't understand. I have real power. And if you ever cross me, I will use it against you.

jokes.text: Boy, she's beautiful, don't you think? I wish I looked like that. - Turn it off. You're making me jealous.
jokes.setup: Susan innocently admiring Marisa Tomei on TV
jokes.punchline: George's guilty overreaction
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - the audience knows why George is uncomfortable while Susan remains oblivious.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: She's so beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? I wasn't looking at her!

jokes.text: The judge! I hate this guy.
jokes.setup: George watching My Cousin Vinny
jokes.punchline: His irrational anger at a fictional character
jokes.explanation: Simple character moment showing George's tendency to get worked up over minor things.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What is he doing? Why would he do that? That makes no sense!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He's an idiot! A complete idiot!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't watch this. I can't watch this anymore.

jokes.text: It's just money. - So when are you getting back from Florida? - Oh, I don't know. I'll play it by ear. Why? - I don't know, just... Things seem a little more exciting when you're around.
jokes.setup: Jerry downplaying his wealth, conversation about his trip
jokes.punchline: Elaine's subtle admission that she finds rich Jerry more appealing
jokes.explanation: Subtle comedy about how money changes relationships. Elaine's attraction to Jerry's wealth is uncomfortably honest.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not rich. I mean, I do okay, but I'm not like, wealthy or anything.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Right, right. Of course not.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I just have a nice apartment and some savings. That's all.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Mmm-hmm. You know what? You're looking really good today.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? I look the same as always.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, there's something different. Something... successful about you.

jokes.text: I'm loving this.
jokes.setup: Jerry watching the angry cable guy confront Kramer
jokes.punchline: His sadistic enjoyment of the cable company's frustration
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Jerry's petty revenge. The simplicity makes it effective.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Look at him. He's actually enjoying this.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The cable guy is finally getting what he deserves.

jokes.text: She's in the hospital. She has an arrhythmia. - What about Marisa Tomei?
jokes.setup: Elaine explaining Katy is hospitalized
jokes.punchline: George's immediate concern for his celebrity connection rather than Katy
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - his complete self-centeredness when someone he barely knows is hospitalized.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Katy's in the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The hospital? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: She had some kind of accident.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Is she gonna be okay?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I don't know, they're not sure yet.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, did she mention me? I mean, when you were talking to her, did she say anything about me?

jokes.text: Look, I got a few good years left. If I want a Chip Ahoy, I'm having it.
jokes.setup: Helen complaining about multiple open cookie boxes
jokes.punchline: Morty's defiant stance on cookie selection in his golden years
jokes.explanation: Excellent old-man defiance moment. The specificity of Chip Ahoy and mortality urgency is perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Morty, why do you have seven open boxes of cookies in this house?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I'm eighty years old, Helen. I want to have choices!

jokes.text: Why can't I buy my father a car? - Your father doesn't need a car. - Yes, I do. - Oh, Morty. - We're keeping it. - Over my dead body.
jokes.setup: Family argument about accepting the Cadillac
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire back-and-forth revealing family dynamics
jokes.explanation: Great family dynamics comedy. The ping-pong nature of the argument is very realistic and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: What do you mean we can't take it? It's a Cadillac!

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Morty, we discussed this. We can't accept gifts like this.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Who cares what we discussed? It's a Cadillac!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Dad, Mom's right. It's too much.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Too much? It's a Cadillac, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: See? This is what I'm talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: What? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: You and that car. You don't even know the man.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I know him. He's a good man. A man with a Cadillac.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not knowing someone, Dad.

jokes.text: Well, this worked out just as I had hoped.
jokes.setup: Parents fighting over his generous gift
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dry commentary on the chaos he's created
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry deadpan in the face of family chaos. The understatement is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I give my parents one gift and suddenly I'm responsible for World War III. My mother thinks the blender is a sign I don't love her cooking. My father thinks it's a sign I'm wasting money. They're fighting over a kitchen appliance like it's the last helicopter out of Saigon.

jokes.text: My seat's got a memory, in case somebody moves it. I could be in prison for years. I come out, my seat goes back to where I like it.
jokes.setup: Morty showing off car features
jokes.punchline: The extreme scenario justification for seat memory
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic. The prison scenario is so specific and unnecessary, making it hilariously over the top.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You see this? Seat memory. My seat adjusts automatically.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What do you need that for?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Well, what if I'm driving down the road, someone else gets in my car and adjusts my seat? Then I get back in, I don't know where I am!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Who's getting in your car?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I don't know! A valet, a thief...

jokes.text: Do I have to take a ride? - He doesn't wanna take a ride. - What do you think, I've never ridden in a Cadillac? Believe me, I have ridden in a Cadillac hundreds of times. - Thousands.
jokes.setup: Jack refusing to ride in Morty's new car
jokes.punchline: His increasingly exaggerated claims of Cadillac experience
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing competitive old-man dynamics. The escalation from hundreds to thousands is perfectly petty.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I'm not getting in that car.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Why not? It's a beautiful Cadillac!

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: A Cadillac? I don't ride in Cadillacs.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? You love Cadillacs!

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I had a friend with a Cadillac once. Terrible experience.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: One friend?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I knew a man, owned seven Cadillacs. Lost everything.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Seven?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Worked at a Cadillac factory for thirty years. They blacklisted me. The whole family.

jokes.text: It's like going steady, you know. Going steady, engaged. It's all just stuff you say.
jokes.setup: George trying to justify meeting Marisa while engaged
jokes.punchline: His dismissal of engagement as meaningless teenage-level commitment
jokes.explanation: Perfect George rationalization. The equation of engagement with going steady shows his immaturity and moral flexibility.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: An engagement? What is an engagement? It's just a thing that people do. It doesn't mean anything. It's like when you're a teenager and you go steady. It's a teenage thing!

jokes.text: Move a pinkie if it's yes. Can you move a pinkie?
jokes.setup: George talking to unconscious Katy in hospital
jokes.punchline: His desperate attempt to get consent for the Marisa setup
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully inappropriate George moment. Trying to get permission from someone in a coma is perfectly George-level awful.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Katy? Katy, can you hear me? I need to ask you something. It's very important. I need your permission to set you up with my friend Marisa. Hello? Katy?

jokes.text: Well, that's funny. Because as it happens... I don't have call waiting. Seems to me that the phone company would know that.
jokes.setup: Fake phone company man trying to gain access
jokes.punchline: Kramer catching him in the lie
jokes.explanation: Good detective work from Kramer. The logical trap is well-constructed and satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm here to check your phone lines.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Phone lines? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: There's been some complaints in the building. I need to check all the connections.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... you're not from the phone company.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Of course I am.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No you're not. You're Kramer from upstairs.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I know who I am!

jokes.text: Hey, McNab! Chunnel's on HBO tonight! Why don't you stop by!
jokes.setup: Kramer putting fake phone company guy on hold
jokes.punchline: His casual invitation to watch HBO together
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer move - turning a scam attempt into a social invitation. The specific movie reference adds authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, hello? Hey, listen, I gotta put you on hold for a second.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey, you wanna watch HBO?

jokes.text: Morty Seinfeld has been stealing funds from the treasury. - Stealing? - What proof do you have? - Proof? You want proof? He's driving around in a brand-new Cadillac.
jokes.setup: Jack's accusation of embezzlement
jokes.punchline: The car as definitive proof of financial crime
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the condo politics. The circular logic of wealth equals guilt is absurdly realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: You embezzled money from the company!

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I did not embezzle anything!

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Then how do you explain that car?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: What car?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: We all saw his act last year at the playhouse. He's lucky he can pay his rent. - Jack's right. He stinks. - It's his material.
jokes.setup: Jack using Jerry's comedy as evidence against Morty
jokes.punchline: The consensus that Jerry's comedy is terrible and Morty's defensive response
jokes.explanation: Great community dynamics and parental defensiveness. Morty blaming the material rather than delivery is perfect dad logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Your son stole my jokes!

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous. My son would never do that.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: He's using them in his act. Look at this material.

dialogue.speaker: Resident
dialogue.line: Yeah, this stuff is terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Resident
dialogue.line: Really bad.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Well, if it's so terrible, how would anyone even notice he stole it?

jokes.text: You could put a fence around these condos and call it an insane asylum. Nobody would know the difference.
jokes.setup: Jerry witnessing the condo politics chaos
jokes.punchline: His assessment of the community's mental state
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor about retirement community dynamics. The visual is vivid and rings true.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I'm starting to think these people aren't just crazy — they're organized crazy. Like they've formed committees.

jokes.text: Not because he thinks you stole the money... but mainly because you never thanked him... for giving you his aisle seat at Freddy Roman's show.
jokes.setup: Explaining Sol's impeachment vote
jokes.punchline: The petty personal grievance driving political decision
jokes.explanation: Perfect small-community politics satire. The specific comedian reference and seat detail make it feel authentic.

dialogue.speaker: Evelyn
dialogue.line: Sol voted to impeach because the President didn't invite him to the state dinner. Can you believe that? A man's entire political career, based on one snub!

jokes.text: We're losing all our friends.
jokes.setup: Not being invited to the Lichtenbergs' party due to the car
jokes.punchline: Helen's summary of their social isolation
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective moment showing the unintended consequences of Jerry's generosity.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: We're not invited to the Lichtenbergs' party. We're not invited anywhere. We're pariahs.

jokes.text: Like she would ever go out with a short, stocky, bald man. Like that's her type. She's an Oscar winner. Besides, I don't even know her. It's not like anyone's trying to fix us up. Who would try and fix me up with Marisa Tomei?
jokes.setup: George talking to Susan about Marisa Tomei
jokes.punchline: His complete reversal while the audience knows the truth
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony. George's complete 180 while Susan (and audience) knows he's lying creates perfect comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Marisa Tomei? She's gorgeous. I'm telling you, I'd do anything to go out with her.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: George, you're married.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But if I wasn't... I mean, come on.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? She's not even that pretty. I don't know what I was thinking. Average at best.

jokes.text: Well, I do love you very much. - And I love you, Marisa. - Well, then. Come on, get dressed. We're going to be late for the premiere.
jokes.setup: George's fantasy sequence with Marisa Tomei
jokes.punchline: The dream scenario playing out in his imagination
jokes.explanation: Standard fantasy sequence that shows George's delusions of grandeur. The premiere detail adds Hollywood glamour.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm out there Jerry and I'm lovin' it.

dialogue.speaker: Marisa Tomei
dialogue.line: You're incredible, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was at this moment, as I was sitting in the coffee shop, that it all became clear to me. This was it. This was the moment my life was going to change.

jokes.text: Stupid foldout. Why do they put the bar in the middle of the bed?
jokes.setup: Jerry dealing with uncomfortable sleeping arrangements
jokes.punchline: Universal complaint about foldout bed design
jokes.explanation: Basic observational humor that's relatable but not particularly original or clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what nobody likes? A foldout bed. Nobody. It's like sleeping on a geometry problem. You're trying to find that one sweet spot where it's not actively trying to fold you in half like a human taco.

jokes.text: I just got off the phone with Marisa Tomei. I just spoke to Marisa Tomei. And I wasn't even that nervous. I can't remember calling a woman without being nervous.
jokes.setup: George's excitement about talking to Marisa
jokes.punchline: His amazement at his own confidence with a celebrity
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing George's confidence boost from celebrity attention. The irony is that he's most comfortable when stakes are highest.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't believe I just talked to Marisa Tomei! Me! I actually spoke to her!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What is happening to me? I'm like a different person. I walked right up to her and I just... I talked!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is amazing. I'm amazing!

jokes.text: I don't have a boyfriend. - She doesn't know that. We say that you do. - It's good. Believe me. - I thought my idea was just as good. - The dentist thing? - Yeah, right. The dentist thing. - The dentist thing was not good.
jokes.setup: George and Elaine crafting an alibi story
jokes.punchline: Their collaborative lie-building process and creative differences
jokes.explanation: Great meta-comedy about crafting deceptions. The creative process of lying becomes its own comedy show.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Okay, so I tell her I was at the movies.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Which movie?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know, something with explosions.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: George, you have to be specific. She'll ask you about the plot.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Fine, fine. A man... blows things up. There's a woman. The woman is in danger from the explosions.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's every movie.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what? It's a good lie!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not a good lie if it could apply to literally anything.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're overcomplicating this. I'll just say I was with you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. We're not alibying each other. That's how you get caught.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what's your brilliant plan?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You were at the dentist.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The dentist? I have no fillings!

jokes.text: Just imports? No exports? - He's an importer-exporter. Okay? - So I'm dating Art Vandelay.
jokes.setup: Creating details for fictional boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Elaine's correction and acceptance of the elaborate lie
jokes.explanation: Good callback to recurring Seinfeld mythology. Art Vandelay's profession evolution is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's a marine biologist.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A marine biologist.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He lives in the woods.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The woods?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's very unique.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Does he have a job?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's a stockbroker.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, wait—he's a lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Which is it?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's a marine biologist who works as a stockbroker and a lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But it works.

jokes.text: They're a dime a dozen. - Oh, hi, Jerry. Mrs. Choate, this is my son, Jerry. - Give me that rye. - Stop it. - Help! Someone help! - Shut up, you old bag. - Thief! Stop him! Stop him, he's got my rye!
jokes.setup: Mrs. Choate's dismissive comment and Jerry's entrance
jokes.punchline: Flashback to Jerry mugging her for marble rye bread
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that creates perfect plot convergence. The coincidence is absurd but dramatically satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Choate
dialogue.line: He mugged me for a marble rye!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I did not mug you for a marble rye.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Choate
dialogue.line: You knocked me down in front of the bakery!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I would never mug anyone for a marble rye. That's ridiculous.

jokes.text: If I was having an affair with Elaine... I wouldn't tell you I'm seeing Elaine. I would make up another person to tell you I would see... and then I would go see Elaine.
jokes.setup: Susan's suspicion about George and Elaine
jokes.punchline: George's elaborate logic about proper affair conduct
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George logic that's both completely wrong and perfectly reasonable. His expertise in deception is accidentally revealed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not an affair if you're not in a relationship. I'm not in a relationship with Susan, so technically it's not an affair.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: We've been dating for two years, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes, but there was never a definitive moment where we agreed to be exclusive. It's all implied. Implied doesn't count.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's insane.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Is it? Or is it the only sane way to live?

jokes.text: I'll get you! I'll get you, Kramer! You won't get away with this.
jokes.setup: Ongoing cable company revenge plot
jokes.punchline: Cable guy's dramatic threat as he presumably leaves defeated
jokes.explanation: Standard villain departure line. Functional for plot resolution but not particularly inventive.

dialogue.speaker: Cable Guy
dialogue.line: You think you've won, but this isn't over. I know where you live. I know your schedule. I'll be back when you least expect it, and your cable will go out at the most crucial moment of your life.

jokes.text: 'Manure' is not really that bad a word. I mean, it's 'newer,' which is good. And a 'ma' in front of it, which is also good. 'Manure.' It's really not that bad.
jokes.setup: George's date conversation with Marisa
jokes.punchline: His bizarre etymology of 'manure' as pickup line material
jokes.explanation: Classic George pseudo-intellectual moment. The wordplay is genuinely clever while being completely inappropriate for a date.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I was thinking about the word 'manure.' It's a good word. It's a funny word. But I was thinking, if it had been a different word, it might have been useful.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Like, if it was 'ma-new.' That would be a great opening line. 'Hey baby, you're ma-new.' Like, you're new to me, you're my new.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But instead we got 'manure.' Man you're. That's not a pickup line, that's a description of what I do for a living.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. I thought that Art wanted to give up the exporting. - What did I say? - The importing. - I did? - So, what does he import? - Chips. - What kind of chips? - Potato. - Some corn. - And what does he export? - Diapers.
jokes.setup: Susan catching inconsistencies in the Art Vandelay story
jokes.punchline: Elaine's increasingly desperate improvisation
jokes.explanation: Great cringe comedy as the lie falls apart. The specific products (chips and diapers) are absurdly random.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Wait, you said Art Vandelay was an architect, but last week you said he was a marine biologist.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He... he changed careers! People do that.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: And you said he lived in New York, but then you mentioned his place in Aruba.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He has two homes! Very successful people have two homes.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Elaine, Art Vandelay doesn't exist, does he?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Of course he exists! I have a picture of him. Well, I had a picture. It got... wet.

jokes.text: Matches. Really long matches.
jokes.setup: Susan asking what Art Vandelay imports
jokes.punchline: George's panicked, oddly specific answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect George panic response. The specificity of 'really long' matches makes it funnier than generic matches.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: What does Art Vandelay import?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Uh, latex. He's a latex salesman.

jokes.text: It's him. It's your son. Now I know where I saw him. He stole my marble rye. - My son never stole anything. He's a good boy. - They should lock him up. Like father, like son.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Choate recognizing Jerry from the mugging
jokes.punchline: The coincidence derailing Morty's impeachment defense
jokes.explanation: Perfect plot convergence where past actions destroy present plans. The 'like father, like son' irony is devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Choate
dialogue.line: You're the one who got mugged!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes, that was me.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Choate
dialogue.line: I was mugged the same day! Same neighborhood!

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: This is incredible. What are the odds?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Pretty good in that neighborhood.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You see? You see what this is? This is a conspiracy. They're setting me up!

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Choate
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Don't you see? You, him—it's all connected. They want to make it look like crime is rampant so they can discredit me before the hearing!

jokes.text: I don't know why we do it. I guess maybe we just kind of enjoy... taking advantage of people. But all that's gonna change. From now on, no more 9 to 12, no more 1 to 5. We'll kind of have appointments.
jokes.setup: Jerry's capitulation to the cable company
jokes.punchline: His complete reversal and promise of reform
jokes.explanation: Jerry's complete defeat is satisfying after his smugness. The admission that they enjoy taking advantage is refreshingly honest.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'll do it. I'll call them back and apologize. I was wrong. I was completely wrong about the cable company.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: From now on, I'm paying my bill on time, I'm being respectful to the customer service representatives, and I'm accepting whatever channels they give me without complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No more fighting the system. No more thinking I'm smarter than the cable company. They win. They always win. I'm done.

tmdb_overview: George becomes obsessed with Marisa Tomei after he's told he's her type; Jerry gives his father a Cadillac.
insight: Cadillac setup episode delivers steady pace with 54 jokes, building toward two-parter payoff.
